paragraphs.body: Like Real People Do

annotations.comment: Love the title and the inspiration.

paragraphs.body: (Based on the song by Hozier)

The marsh sprawled underneath her and all around, like honey over shattered glass. Spanish moss curled from the arms of cypress and swamp tupelo, weaving quiet passage in the shadows of outstretched arms for all the creatures of the night to slip unseen from tree to tree. It was an ancient place– the type that births monsters into periphery, where banshees and whisps lurk in darkness, where silence falls on heavy shoulders with a weight as if one were holding up the sky itself. She moved carefully, through fog and stagnant water, climbing twisted roots of half-drowned swamp-oak and sweetgum. With every step, the wet earth curled around her ankles like pleading hands, desperately whispering to turn back, or perhaps, she thought, urging her forward. It was as if the marsh itself sensed the wooden stake hidden against her breast, steady as a second heartbeat.

annotations.comment: I love the atmosphere here. I did think you might have gone on a smidgen too long given that this is flash fiction.
annotations.context_left: in darkness, where silence falls on heavy shoulders with a weight as if one were holding up the sky
annotations.context_right: . She moved carefully, through fog and stagnant water, climbing twisted roots of half-drowned swamp-

paragraphs.body: Somewhere deep on the horizon behind her lay the small, quiet village, sleeping under the weight of old wives’ tales and bedtime stories, warnings curling in the air like smoke. Legends of the wretched things that lived in the marsh were echoed from generation to generation, and yet, here she was, village lights so dim they could have been fireflies. Spade and rope in hand, she ventured further into the night, feeling both as if she were being followed, and more alone than she had ever known.

annotations.comment: I’m very interested in her and what this situation is.

paragraphs.body: After a time, she stopped, and turned her eyes upward at a tangled swamp willow. The roots spilled out from the ground like veins, thick with wild rye and switchgrass swaying in the warm night air. The ground was soft here, gentle and loose, as if something had been moving beneath it. Channels and streams seemed to stretch around this place, rounding unusual patterns, carving out nearby hillsides to nothing; this was a place the marsh had not yet swallowed. She dug.

annotations.comment: A really small point but I think I missed when she got to drier land — I was picturing her in the swamp.

paragraphs.body: She dug beneath the peat moss, through clay seams and roots, the scent of earth rich and damp in the air. It was thick against her lungs– her hands growing tired. Earthworms twisted in loosened soil, millipedes curled into themselves and disappeared into the dark. The stars moved overhead, reflected and rippled in the water, killifish darted in the shadows. Time moved slowly.

annotations.comment: Another small point but I wondered why she didn’t bring a tool? Since this was a job she clearly prepared for.

paragraphs.body: The first thing she unearthed was a hand– pale and frost-kissed, as light as swamp-milkweed, too perfect, too still. Then, a wrist, then an arm, and at last, she uncovered a face. Skin pale in silvery moonlight, streaked with dirt, lips slightly parted, caught between breath and prayer. She lay unmoving, still. Sleeping, perhaps, or in a place between sleep and death, somewhere she could not quite understand. The creature in the earth did not look like a monster.

annotations.comment: I suggest giving the corpse a name — two “she”s is going to be confusing.
Like, here, I unfortunately cannot tell which she can’t understand this.
annotations.context_right: unearthed was a hand– pale and frost-kissed, as light as swamp-milkweed, too perfect, too still. Th

paragraphs.body: Where she should have seen death and rot, spine twisted, sunken face, nails long and blood-stained, she saw only a girl.

When she opened her eyes, she was beautiful, in a peculiar sort of way. Her features were delicate, almost gentle, forgiving, but her skin too smooth, eyes too dark; light refusing to catch in them, reflecting nothing but the dim glow of the stars. She moved with the grace of someone who had forgotten how to move and peered through strings of mud-caked hair. And yet, there was nothing monstrous about her. Not in the way the legends told.

Where the thing should have lunged at her, arms outstretched, nails sharp and curled, where she should have pulled the stake from her chest and pushed it through the thing’s heart, where she should have run back to her village, a hero, a champion who killed the beast, she didn’t.

annotations.comment: I would like to know just a smidgen more about why this woman (?) set out to find and unearth this girl on this particular night.

paragraphs.body: Instead, she simply lifted the girl from the earth, pulling her into a world that did not know who, or what, she was. A world that no longer remembered her name, or who she had been. Only legends, stories, warnings, passed through time. She lifted her head, took in the blackness of air and swayed unsteadily in her arms, weightless. For a moment, she thought she saw something flash in the creature’s mouth, but the moon recoiled into clouds, light shifted, and it was gone. The wind moved through rose-mallow, petals ghostly in the moonlight.  The air between them hung thick and uncertain.  She had no language to say it, no words to give to the thing.

annotations.comment: I am being annoyingly realistic, sorry, but how is she able to see all of this?

paragraphs.body: Her body ached. There was dirt beneath her nails, smudging her arms, pressed into creases of her skin, clinging to her and around her. Muscles weighted in exhaustion, heart heavy and tired. Her spade lay discarded behind a tangle of salt grass, blade dull. She did not ask why the creature had been buried. The creature did not ask why she had been found.

annotations.text: She did not ask why the creature had been buried. The creature did not ask why she had been found.
annotations.comment: I’m confused about the main character’s want here — it seemed to me like she went on this journey specifically to find this girl, and had some idea of where she was. Am I wrong about that? Or does seeing the girl actually change her in some way?

paragraphs.body: She only looked at her accidental savior, the one who had uncovered her with something between gratitude and sadness. She reached out a tender hand, dirt-streaked fingers outstretched to cup her cheek. It was warm and alive. She shivered, but did not pull away. The thing’s body was too light, too fragile for something monstrous. But there was something in the way that she watched her; something too knowing, too sad.
A smile that barely curled at the edges. "I knew that look, dear. Eyes always seeking. Was there in someone that dug long ago."

annotations.comment: I don’t know who’s speaking here.

paragraphs.body: Her fingers drifted lower, skimming over the place where she had hidden the stake. Not in accusation, just recognition. Understanding.
"So I will not ask you why you were creeping," she murmured. "In some sad way, I already know."

The girl in her arms did not let go. She barely breathed. The night clung to her where the earth had held her too long in its cold, quiet grasp. The wind wove through the cypress; it curled around them; the marsh signing, remembering. The first rays of the dawn slipped between the branches of the willow, painting the water in pale gold. Light spilled onto the soil and moved in the spaces between them.  A heron stirred in the distance.

She should have left her there. She should have buried her back beneath the roots and let the earth reclaim what it had already swallowed. But the girl was watching her, watching her like she had never seen another thing alive before, watching like she could not believe she had been pulled from the dark.
And then, a voice like breath on glass, something so fragile it might break, the girl whispered,
“Honey, just put your sweet lips on my lips. We should just kiss like real people do.”
The words were weightless; mist over still water. The sky brightened, and the marsh drank in the morning light.

annotations.comment: I really enjoyed this story, Sophia. It’s spectral and creepy but also lovely. I could see the world you evoked and found it transfixing. The ending didn’t land for me — I felt you were referencing the song too directly and while I did listen to it, it didn’t feel concrete enough to understand what happens here. In general, I think you have aced atmosphere and poetic language but need to work a bit on conflict and plot (as we all do, no worries). So I’d pose my questions to you — what does your main character want? It seems to me that she wants to find and kill this vampire/girl for reasons I don’t find out. But then when she actually sees the girl, she can’t do it. And the girl maybe transforms her? It’s not a bad start but we need more information to follow — why go after this girl at all? Why fall prey to her, if that’s what happens? How is the girl able to manipulate her? Thinking through those answers will be helpful. Also, as noted, I would name one or both of these. It was confusing following two “she”s I’m sorry to say! Great work.
